title: Wall Street Is Taking Control of Bitcoin
transcript: Imagine a silent war being waged for control  over the base layer of the world's most important   cryptocurrency. For months, Black Rockck has been  the undisputed king of the Bitcoin ETF market,   hoarding over 52.7 billion in assets under  management. But a new regulatory filing has   just revealed that another Wall Street titan  is preparing to launch a direct assault on that   empire. and their strategy threatens to completely  monopolize the remaining liquid supply of Bitcoin.   My name is Guy and you're watching the Coin  Bureau. So then, why is Morgan Stanley suddenly   building its own native Bitcoin infrastructure?  Is this the ultimate validation of the digital   gold thesis? Or is Wall Street preparing to  completely centralize the underlying supply   of a decentralized network? Before we get into  all of that though, let's look at the filing that   just sent shock waves through the institutional  crypto space. On the 4th of March 2026, Morgan   Stanley officially filed a critical amendment to  its spot Bitcoin ETF application with the SEC. The   mainstream financial press covered this as just  another late entrant to an already crowded ETF   market. They pointed out that Black Rockck's iBit  already dominates the space, commanding nearly 60%   of all US spot Bitcoin ETF assets. The surface  level narrative is clean and simple. Morgan   Stanley manages roughly $8.2 trillion in client  assets, and they're simply trying to capture a few   basis points of management fees from their wealthy  clients instead of rooting them to BlackRock.   But if you read the actual architecture of their  proposal, they're planning something much more   aggressive. And let's be precise about what is  actually happening. The amended filing reveals   a dual custody model. Coinbase custody will  handle the onchain security of the Bitcoin in   offline cold wallets. Meanwhile, BNY Melon,  the 200-year-old traditional banking giant,   will handle the cash administration and transfer  agency. Now, Morgan Stanley is not just launching   a fund. They're attempting to build a closed loop  ecosystem. Unlike other wealth managers who simply   allocate client capital into Black Rockck's IBIT,  Morgan Stanley wants to own the entire stack. And   to understand why this matters, you have to look  at what they just filed 2 weeks prior. On the 18th   of February, Morgan Stanley applied to the office  of the controller of the currency for a national   trust bank charter. The proposed entity is called  Morgan Stanley Digital Trust. And it's not just a   regulatory box ticking exercise. The firm's head  of digital asset strategy, Amy Oldenberg, publicly   declared that they are building native custody  and exchange infrastructure. Her exact words were   that Morgan Stanley can no longer just rent the  technology from third parties. And here is how it   works. Right now, the entire US spot Bitcoin ETF  market relies almost exclusively on one company.   Coinbase acts as the custodian for over 80% of  the assets within these institutional vehicles.   If multiple trillion dollar asset managers all  rely on the exact same cryptonative custodian,   you get a massive concentration of systemic risk.  Morgan Stanley is deliberately preparing to bypass   that Coinbase dominated custody monopoly. By  securing a federal trust charter and building   their own native vaults, they're positioning  themselves to directly hoard base layer Bitcoin   on behalf of their clients rather than relying on  third-party custodians. They are building the back   office plumbing of blockchain finance entirely  inhouse. And speaking of staying ahead of these   massive institutional power plays before they hit  the mainstream headlines, this is exactly why you   need the Coinb Telegram channel in your life.  This is where we share the latest breaking news,   provide deep dive alpha, and give you the most  important market updates, all pinged directly   to your device so you don't miss a thing. Whether  it's a hidden SEC filing or a major shift in ETF   flow data, our members see it first. So, sign up  today using the link down below or by scanning   the QR code on the screen. All right, now back  to the vaults because the implications of this   infrastructure war are profound for the actual  price discovery of Bitcoin. Analysts project that   Morgan Stanley's aggressive entry combined with  their massive retail distribution network through   Erade could push total US spot ETF assets to $220  billion by the end of 2026. But to understand the   true scale of that number, we have to look at  the maths of Bitcoin's liquid supply. There are   currently roughly 19.97 million Bitcoin already  mined. But according to blockchain forensics,   between 3 and 4 million of those coins are  lost forever. Furthermore, Glass node data   shows that approximately 13.5 million Bitcoin  are held by illquid entities who simply refuse   to sell. That leaves a freely tradable float  of roughly 3.9 million Bitcoin. And the mining   network only produces 450 new Bitcoin per day.  When multiple trillion dollar asset managers like   Black Rockck and Morgan Stanley begin competing  to custody the base layer, you get a structural   supply shock. And we've seen this exact scenario  play out in traditional finance before. When the   SPDR Gold Trust or GLD launched in late 2004, it  completely transformed the investment demand for   physical gold. The price of gold surged nearly  287% over the following 8 years as institutional   vaults absorbed the available supply. Bitcoin  is currently experiencing that exact same   financialization process, but on a mathematically  strictly scarce asset. This is undeniably bullish   for the price. But here is the bitter irony at  the center of all this. This historic supply   shock means that the vast majority of Bitcoin's  future liquidity will be locked inside regulated   Wall Street controlled vaults. If you control all  your keys, it's your Bitcoin. If you don't control   your keys, it's not your Bitcoin. Simple as. The  decentralized peer-to-peer network is quietly   being transformed into a traditional banking  reserve asset. So Morgan Stanley's entry into   the Bitcoin custody and ETF space is not just a  late grab for management fees. It's a declaration   of war against Black Rockck's dominance. It's  a strategic move to secure direct ownership of   the underlying asset and control the plumbing  of digital finance. The institutional adoption   phase is over. We are now in the institutional  monopolization phase. And while this fierce   competition will undoubtedly drive massive capital  inflows and create a historic supply shock,   the cost is the rapid centralization of Bitcoin's  physical custody. Wall Street is no longer just   adopting crypto, but building the vaults to  lock it away. And I'm not sure that's what   Satoshi had in mind all those years ago. But  what do you think? Is Morgan Stanley's push   into native custody bullish for Bitcoin's price  or terrifying for its decentralization? Let me   know your thoughts in the comments down below.  And if you want to understand exactly how Black   Rockck's ETF plumbing works behind the scenes,  well, you could check out our deep dive right   over here. Thank you all so much for watching and  I'll see you again very soon. This is Guy signing
summary_hu: A Wall Street-i intézmények, mint a Morgan Stanley és a BlackRock, hatalmi harcot vívnak a Bitcoin piacon, különös figyelmet fordítva a Bitcoin ETF-ekre és az infrastruktúrára. A Morgan Stanley legújabb lépései, mint a saját Bitcoin tárolási és kereskedési infrastruktúra kiépítése, arra utalnak, hogy a cég a BlackRock dominanciájával szembeni versenyt generál. Az új ETF-jük, valamint a nemzeti bizalmi bank létesítési kérelmük révén a Morgan Stanley egy olyan zárt ökoszisztémát kíván létrehozni, amely közvetlenül kezeli a Bitcoin-vagyonokat, elkerülve a harmadik fél szolgáltatókat. Ez a központosított modell a Bitcoin decentralizált természetét fenyegeti, és súlyos hatással lehet a piacra, mivel a hedge fundok versenyzése a Bitcoin tárolásáért mérsékli a piacon elérhető likviditást.
summary_en: Wall Street institutions, notably Morgan Stanley and BlackRock, are in a power struggle over the Bitcoin market, focusing on Bitcoin ETFs and infrastructure. Morgan Stanley's recent moves, such as building its own Bitcoin custody and trading infrastructure, suggest a competitive approach against BlackRock's dominance. Through their new ETF application and proposal for a national trust bank, Morgan Stanley aims to create a closed-loop ecosystem that directly manages Bitcoin assets, bypassing third-party custodians. This centralized model threatens the decentralized nature of Bitcoin and could significantly impact the market as competing hedge funds vie for custody, restricting the available liquidity.
key_points_hu: A Morgan Stanley saját Bitcoin ökoszisztémát épít, elkerülve a Coinbase monopóliumát.
A Bitcoin készlet központosítása a hagyományos bankok által fenyegeti a decentralizációs elvet.
A Fordítóárfolyam mérése és a jövőbeli likviditás korlátozása elindíthatja a Bitcoin árának szakszistematikus növekedését.
key_points_en: Morgan Stanley is building its own Bitcoin ecosystem, circumventing Coinbase's monopoly.
The centralization of Bitcoin custody by traditional banks threatens the principle of decentralization.
Measuring the monetary flow and potential future liquidity restrictions could trigger systematic price increases for Bitcoin.
